campaigns.name: Marketing Agency Owners — Batch 1
campaigns.label: ReRev Outreach / MarketingAgency_Batch1
campaigns.cta_model: Offer to set up the 5-minute follow-up automation on a brief call. Call is the filter.
campaigns.hyperlink_instruction: Before sending each email, highlight 'marketing agency owners' in the body and hyperlink it to https://rerev.io/agencies. That is the only link needed.
campaigns.email_template_version: v2 — automation offer CTA (updated from v1 consult CTA)
campaigns.notes: 14 drafts created 2026-04-21 via Gmail MCP. Label must be applied manually in Gmail — search in:drafts then bulk-select and label.

contacts.name: Megan Shroy
contacts.subject: Your posts on AI transparency at Approach
contacts.body:
Hi Megan,

Your LinkedIn posts arguing that agencies should be upfront with clients about how and when they use AI are the ones I keep returning to. You framed disclosure as a trust practice rather than a marketing stance, and Approach earning Ragan Agency of the Year 2025 while holding that line says the market rewards it. That sequencing, getting the ethics of AI use settled before the automation itself, is close to how I think about my work.

I am Keyona, the founder of ReRev Labs. ReRev Labs specializes in using AI-powered automation to give founders their time back without sacrificing the integrity of their voice or their work. In the past that has looked like:

* 5-minute automated follow-up (transcript to Claude drafts with scope log to your Gmail drafts)
* Claude-native second brain that diminishes the pain of context switching between clients
* Client reporting plus scope tracking (client data plus SOW plus week's activity to Claude synthesizes to client-ready report plus internal scope-delta view)

Among other builds. I work with marketing agency owners who want that kind of leverage inside their own operations without risking what makes their client work strong.

One of the builds I listed, the 5-minute follow-up automation, is something I set up for clients directly. If any of this resonates, I would love to get on a brief call and walk you through what that setup looks like for an agency your size. If it is a fit, I can have it running for you the same week.

Warmly, Keyona

contacts.name: David Ebner
contacts.subject: Your episode on authenticity as the agency edge
contacts.body:
Hi David,

Your episode 335 on The Innovative Agency about authenticity being the agency edge in a sea of AI content landed for me, especially the framing that storytelling is not a layer added on top of AI output but the thing that decides whether the output is worth publishing at all. Acquiring Lush Concepts to extend storytelling into every digital surface is a clear operational signal of that belief. The order of operations you described, craft first and automation in service of it, is the one I build around.

I am Keyona, the founder of ReRev Labs. ReRev Labs specializes in using AI-powered automation to give founders their time back without sacrificing the integrity of their voice or their work. In the past that has looked like:

contacts.name: Jackie Hermes
contacts.subject: Your posts on bootstrapping Accelity
contacts.body:
Hi Jackie,

Your daily LinkedIn posts on what it actually takes to bootstrap a B2B SaaS agency are the ones I find myself saving, especially the unfiltered operational side, lead segmentation calls, private label threats you are tracking, how you think about cash flow without outside funding. It reads like a public working log, which is the version of thought leadership I trust most. That orientation, building the business and documenting it in real time, is close to how I think about my work.

I am Keyona, the founder of ReRev Labs. ReRev Labs specializes in using AI-powered automation to give founders their time back without sacrificing the integrity of their voice or their work. In the past that has looked like:

contacts.name: Christy Hiler
contacts.subject: Your episode on succession under pressure
contacts.body:
Hi Christy,

Your episode with Whit on The Innovative Agency about succession under pressure sat with me, in particular your framing of becoming sole owner as a mindset shift about what ownership itself can look like rather than just a title change. The part about protecting culture and continuing to grow through the transition is the piece I find hardest to operationalize in the work I do. That sequencing, getting the foundation right before the public story catches up, is how I approach my work.

I am Keyona, the founder of ReRev Labs. ReRev Labs specializes in using AI-powered automation to give founders their time back without sacrificing the integrity of their voice or their work. In the past that has looked like:

contacts.name: Valerie Jennings
contacts.subject: Your article on AI systems outperforming traditional campaigns
contacts.body:
Hi Valerie,

Your 2026 trends piece on why AI systems will outperform traditional campaigns is the article I have been pointing people to, especially the framing that AI visibility is a practice built into a campaign from the start rather than retrofitted afterward. Twenty-plus years running JSMM and your DeepLearning.AI certification give the argument technical credibility without the hype. That way of treating AI as infrastructure rather than a feature is close to how I think about my work.

contacts.name: Jenny Cross
contacts.subject: Your decade of building TwoTone
contacts.body:
Hi Jenny,

The values you lead TwoTone by, remarkable, relational, resourceful, show up in how you structure your fractional CMO engagements rather than only in how the agency talks about itself, which is part of why a decade of growth to two Inc. 5000 placements reads as intentional. Pairing that model with deep client relationships means you are effectively operationalizing the agency's own judgment for companies that cannot afford a full executive. That orientation, where operations carry the values rather than copy, is close to how I think about my work.

I am Keyona, the founder of ReRev Labs. ReRev Labs specializes in using AI-powered automation to give founders their time back without sacrificing the integrity of their voice or their work. In the past that has looked like:

contacts.name: Natalie Eisenberg
contacts.subject: Your post on 2026 as the year of the Indie Agency
contacts.body:
Hi Natalie,

Your framing that 2026 is the year of the Indie Agency, tied to a fundamental shift in what brand and marketing leaders actually want from their partners, is the read of the market I find most useful right now. The observation that clients are moving toward partners who can hold both strategy and execution without a layered hand-off is something I see play out in the operational drag inside smaller firms. That diagnosis, that indie advantage is real but only shows up if the back office can carry the weight, is close to how I think about my work.

contacts.name: Lyn Wineman
contacts.subject: Your 200-plus episodes of Agency for Change
contacts.body:
Hi Lyn,

Watching KidGlov become one of the first B Corp agencies in Nebraska and then hosting 200-plus episodes of Agency for Change is the kind of long-arc build that only compounds with time. The podcast reads like a working thesis for the agency, amplifying changemakers as both the product and the marketing, and it lines up with the kind of purpose-operational integrity that clients can feel when they work with you. That approach, where how the agency runs and who it serves are the same answer, is close to how I think about my work.

contacts.name: Anne Zizzo
contacts.subject: Your 30th anniversary and the Celtic Inc. acquisition
contacts.body:
Hi Anne,

Reaching the 30-year mark at Zizzo Group and closing the Celtic Inc. acquisition, your seventh, is the kind of compounding growth that only works if the operational spine can absorb each integration without losing what made the agency itself. The ZG Content Factory launch on top of that reads like you are betting the next decade on operational leverage rather than headcount. That sequencing, acquire carefully and then tool the backend to handle it, is close to how I think about my work.

contacts.name: Aya Kikimova
contacts.subject: Your episode on profitable agency work for small businesses
contacts.body:
Hi Aya,

Your episode 325 on The Innovative Agency about building a profitable innovative marketing practice while serving small and midsize businesses with high-quality work but lean resources is the conversation I have been wanting someone to have out loud. The GPS Method piece on determining marketing readiness in fifteen minutes puts real operational shape around the usual agency-fit conversation, and your roadmap around proprietary AI-driven tools for small businesses says you are building rather than only strategizing. That orientation, where the tooling carries the margin so the service can serve, is close to how I think about my work.

contacts.name: Lydia Michael
contacts.subject: Your book Brand Love and the Blended advisory board
contacts.body:
Hi Lydia,

Brand Love is a marketing book that actually teaches emotional infrastructure rather than campaign mechanics, and pairing that with the new multicultural advisory board at Blended Collective is the moment of the book becoming operational inside the agency itself. The shift from writing about brand-consumer connection to building it into how the agency advises clients is the operational version of the book, which is the step I find most valuable. That throughline, where the thinking and the operations are the same work, is close to how I think about my own.

contacts.name: Andy Crestodina
contacts.subject: Your AI-Search Adoption Survey 2026
contacts.body:
Hi Andy,

Your AI-Search Adoption Survey this year, especially the data point that 44 percent of respondents say AI Chat tools have changed how they look for information online and 55 percent now use AI Chat as their primary or frequent research tool, is the benchmark I have been using to ground conversations with founders. The annual cadence of Orbit's original research and the 65-to-95 percent climb in content marketer AI adoption over two years gives the story a longitudinal grounding that is useful for decision-makers. That approach, where the research is the argument rather than the marketing for the argument, is close to how I think about my work.

I am Keyona, the founder of ReRev Labs. ReRev Labs specializes in using AI-powered automation to give founders their time back without sacrificing the integrity of their voice or their work. In the past that has looked like:

contacts.name: Greg Beauchamp
contacts.subject: Your episode on mid-funnel magic and human storytelling
contacts.body:
Hi Greg,

Your AW360 conversation on mid-funnel magic and the art of human storytelling is the episode I keep recommending, especially the part about storytelling earning its place inside measurable performance rather than sitting above it. Pairing an Emmy-winning filmmaker's craft with Bindery's production and measurement apparatus is the operational move that makes the argument real for the agency itself. That integration, craft and measurement built into the same workflow, is close to how I think about my work.

contacts.name: Lee Odden
contacts.subject: Your Beyond B2B podcast on AI, creativity, and influence
contacts.body:
Hi Lee,

Your Beyond B2B episodes on the role creativity and influencer marketing play in humanizing B2B, alongside practical uses of AI for impactful content, are the ones I have been sending to agency founders trying to think about AI without abandoning their craft. The framing that credible voices and simplified complex ideas are the human work while AI amplifies their reach is a clean operational split. That separation, AI serving craft rather than replacing it, is close to how I think about my work.
